From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Overlap (road)](/w/index.php?title=Overlap_(road)&redirect=no "Overlap (road)"))

Road bearing more than one route number

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/2021-10-27_13_22_04_View_north_along_Interstate_99_and_U.S._Route_220_just_north_of_Exit_41_in_Antis_Township%2C_Blair_County%2C_Pennsylvania.jpg/250px-2021-10-27_13_22_04_View_north_along_Interstate_99_and_U.S._Route_220_just_north_of_Exit_41_in_Antis_Township%2C_Blair_County%2C_Pennsylvania.jpg)](/wiki/File:2021-10-27_13_22_04_View_north_along_Interstate_99_and_U.S._Route_220_just_north_of_Exit_41_in_Antis_Township,_Blair_County,_Pennsylvania.jpg)

[Highway shields](/wiki/Highway_shield "Highway shield") on a two-way concurrency, where [I-99](/wiki/Interstate_99 "Interstate 99") and [US 220](/wiki/U.S._Route_220 "U.S. Route 220") share one roadway traveling northbound in [Antis Township, Blair County, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Antis_Township,_Blair_County,_Pennsylvania "Antis Township, Blair County, Pennsylvania")

In a road network, a **concurrency** is an instance of one physical [roadway](/wiki/Carriageway "Carriageway") bearing two or more different [route numbers](/wiki/Route_number "Route number").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The practice is often economically and practically advantageous when multiple routes must pass between a single mountain crossing or over a bridge, or through a major city, and can be accommodated by a single right-of-way. Each route number is typically posted on highways signs where concurrencies are allowed, while some jurisdictions simplify signage by posting one priority route number on highway signs. In the latter circumstance, other route numbers disappear when the concurrency begins and reappear when it ends. In most cases, each route in a concurrency is recognized by maps and atlases.

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concurrency_(road)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

When two roadways share the same [right-of-way](/wiki/Right-of-way_(transportation) "Right-of-way (transportation)"), it is sometimes called a **common section** or **commons**.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Other terminology for a concurrency includes **overlap**,[[3]](#cite_note-3) **coincidence**,[[4]](#cite_note-4) **duplex** (two concurrent routes), **triplex** (three concurrent routes), **multiplex** (any number of concurrent routes),[[5]](#cite_note-m-plex-5) **dual routing** or **triple routing**.[[6]](#cite_note-1982memo-6)[[7]](#cite_note-1983memo-7)

## Concept

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concurrency_(road)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Concept")]

Most concurrencies are simply a combination of at least two route numbers on the same physical roadway. This is often practically advantageous as well as economically advantageous; it may be better for two route numbers to be combined into one along rivers or through mountain valleys. Some countries allow for concurrencies to occur, however, others specifically do not allow it to happen. In those nations which do permit concurrencies, it can become very common. In these countries, there are a variety of concurrences which can occur.

An example of this is the concurrency of [Interstate 70](/wiki/Interstate_70_in_Pennsylvania "Interstate 70 in Pennsylvania") (I-70) and [I-76](/wiki/Interstate_76_(Ohio%E2%80%93New_Jersey)#Pennsylvania "Interstate 76 (Ohio–New Jersey)") on the [Pennsylvania Turnpike](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Turnpike "Pennsylvania Turnpike") between [New Stanton](/wiki/New_Stanton,_Pennsylvania "New Stanton, Pennsylvania") and [Breezewood](/wiki/Breezewood,_Pennsylvania "Breezewood, Pennsylvania"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"). I-70 merges with the Pennsylvania Turnpike so the route number can ultimately continue east into Maryland; instead of having a second physical highway built to carry the route, it is combined with the Pennsylvania Turnpike and the I-76 designation.[[8]](#cite_note-PennDOT10-8) The longest Interstate Highway concurrency is [I-80](/wiki/Interstate_80 "Interstate 80") and [I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90 "Interstate 90"), which run concurrent on the [Indiana Toll Road](/wiki/Indiana_Toll_Road "Indiana Toll Road") and [Ohio Turnpike](/wiki/Ohio_Turnpike "Ohio Turnpike") for 278 miles (447 km) between [Lake Station, Indiana](/wiki/Lake_Station,_Indiana "Lake Station, Indiana") and [Elyria Township, Ohio](/wiki/Elyria_Township,_Ohio "Elyria Township, Ohio").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

There are at least two examples of eight-way concurrencies. The first example is in Indianapolis, between exits 46 and 47 of the 53-mile (85 km) [I-465](/wiki/Interstate_465 "Interstate 465") [beltway](/wiki/Beltway "Beltway"), where the highway is concurrent with [I-69](/wiki/Interstate_69_in_Indiana "Interstate 69 in Indiana"), [U.S. Highway 31](/wiki/U.S._Route_31_in_Indiana "U.S. Route 31 in Indiana") (US 31), [US 36](/wiki/U.S._Route_36_in_Indiana "U.S. Route 36 in Indiana"), [US 40](/wiki/U.S._Route_40_in_Indiana "U.S. Route 40 in Indiana"), [US 52](/wiki/U.S._Route_52_in_Indiana "U.S. Route 52 in Indiana"), [US 421](/wiki/U.S._Route_421_in_Indiana "U.S. Route 421 in Indiana"), and [State Road 67](/wiki/Indiana_State_Road_67 "Indiana State Road 67").[[10]](#cite_note-INDOTmap-10) The second example is in [downtown Athens, Georgia](/wiki/Downtown_Athens_(Georgia) "Downtown Athens (Georgia)"), between exits 4 and 8 of [SR 10 Loop](/wiki/Georgia_State_Route_10_Loop_(Athens) "Georgia State Route 10 Loop (Athens)"), where the highway is concurrent with [US 29](/wiki/U.S._Route_29_in_Georgia "U.S. Route 29 in Georgia"), [US 78](/wiki/U.S._Route_78_in_Georgia "U.S. Route 78 in Georgia"), [US 129](/wiki/U.S._Route_129_in_Georgia "U.S. Route 129 in Georgia"), [US 441](/wiki/U.S._Route_441_in_Georgia "U.S. Route 441 in Georgia"), [SR 8](/wiki/Georgia_State_Route_8 "Georgia State Route 8"), [SR 15](/wiki/Georgia_State_Route_15 "Georgia State Route 15"), and the unsigned SR 422.[[11]](#cite_note-GDOTAthensmap-11)

### Wrong-way concurrency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concurrency_(road)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Wrong-way concurrency")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/OKC_wrongway.svg/250px-OKC_wrongway.svg.png)](/wiki/File:OKC_wrongway.svg)

An example of a wrong-way concurrency in [Oklahoma City](/wiki/Oklahoma_City,_Oklahoma "Oklahoma City, Oklahoma"), Oklahoma; the wrong-way concurrency is highlighted in red.

Since highways in the United States and Canada are usually signed with assigned [cardinal directions](/wiki/Cardinal_direction "Cardinal direction") based on their primary orientation, it is possible for a stretch of roadway shared between two highways to be signed with conflicting, even opposite, cardinal directions in a **wrong-way concurrency**.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] For example, near [Wytheville, Virginia](/wiki/Wytheville,_Virginia "Wytheville, Virginia"), there is a concurrency between [Interstate 77](/wiki/Interstate_77_in_Virginia "Interstate 77 in Virginia") (which runs primarily north–south, as it is signed) and [Interstate 81](/wiki/Interstate_81_in_Virginia "Interstate 81 in Virginia") (which runs primarily northeast–southwest, but is also signed north–south). A vehicle might simultaneously be on I-77 northbound and I-81 southbound, while actually traveling due west.[[12]](#cite_note-VDOT12-12) An unusual example of a three-directional concurrency occurs southeast of [Rhinelander, Wisconsin](/wiki/Rhinelander,_Wisconsin "Rhinelander, Wisconsin"), where [US 8](/wiki/U.S._Route_8 "U.S. Route 8") westbound (the actual compass direction) converges with southbound [Wisconsin Highway 17](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_17 "Wisconsin Highway 17") and northbound [Wisconsin Highway 47](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_47 "Wisconsin Highway 47"), and vice-versa.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Effect on exit numbers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concurrency_(road)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Effect on exit numbers")]

Often when two routes with [exit numbers](/wiki/Exit_number "Exit number") overlap, one of the routes has its exit numbers dominate over the other and can sometimes result in having two exits of the same number, albeit far from each other along the same highway. An example of this is from the concurrency of [I-94](/wiki/Interstate_94_in_Michigan "Interstate 94 in Michigan") and [US 127](/wiki/U.S._Route_127_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 127 in Michigan") near [Jackson](/wiki/Jackson,_Michigan "Jackson, Michigan"), Michigan. The concurrent section of freeway has an exit with [M-106](/wiki/M-106_(Michigan_highway) "M-106 (Michigan highway)"), which is numbered exit 139 using I-94's mileage-based numbers. US 127 also has another exit 139 with the southern end of the [US 127 business loop](/wiki/U.S._Route_127_Business_(Mt._Pleasant,_Michigan) "U.S. Route 127 Business (Mt. Pleasant, Michigan)") in [Mount Pleasant](/wiki/Mount_Pleasant,_Michigan "Mount Pleasant, Michigan"), Michigan. (US 127's [mile markers](/wiki/Mile_marker "Mile marker") in Michigan reflect the cumulative distance north of the Ohio state line; the numbers resume north of the I-94 overlap and reflect the distance accumulated on that concurrency.)[[14]](#cite_note-MDOT13-14)

However, there are also instances where the dominant exit number range is far more than the secondary route's highest exit number, for example the [concurrency of I-75 and I-85](/wiki/Downtown_Connector "Downtown Connector") in [Atlanta, Georgia](/wiki/Atlanta,_Georgia "Atlanta, Georgia")—where [I-75](/wiki/Interstate_75_in_Georgia "Interstate 75 in Georgia") is dominant—the exit numbers range from 242 to 251, while [I-85's](/wiki/Interstate_85_in_Georgia "Interstate 85 in Georgia") highest independent [mile marker](/wiki/Mile_marker "Mile marker") in [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(U.S._state) "Georgia (U.S. state)") is 179.[[15]](#cite_note-GDOT11-12-15)

## Alternatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concurrency_(road)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Alternatives")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/9f/NJ_guide_sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:NJ_guide_sign.jpg)

[U.S. Route 1/9](/wiki/U.S._Route_1/9 "U.S. Route 1/9") concurrency signed on a shield on the [Pulaski Skyway](/wiki/Pulaski_Skyway "Pulaski Skyway")

Some brief concurrencies in the past have been eliminated by reassigning the designations along the roadways. This can involve scaling back the terminus of one designation to the end of a concurrent section. At the same time, there could be an extension of another highway designation that is used to replace the newly shortened designation with another one.

Between states, [US 27](/wiki/U.S._Route_27_in_Michigan "U.S. Route 27 in Michigan") in Michigan previously ran concurrently with [I-69](/wiki/Interstate_69_in_Michigan "Interstate 69 in Michigan") from the Michigan–Indiana state line to the [Lansing, Michigan](/wiki/Lansing,_Michigan "Lansing, Michigan"), area. From there it turned northwards to its terminus at [Grayling](/wiki/Grayling,_Michigan "Grayling, Michigan"). In 1999, the [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan_Department_of_Transportation "Michigan Department of Transportation") and [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana_Department_of_Transportation "Indiana Department of Transportation") departments of transportation petitioned the [American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials](/wiki/American_Association_of_State_Highway_and_Transportation_Officials "American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials") for permission to truncate US 27 at [Fort Wayne, Indiana](/wiki/Fort_Wayne,_Indiana "Fort Wayne, Indiana").[[16]](#cite_note-SCRN99-16) In 2002, Michigan removed the US 27 designation from I-69 and extended the [US 127](/wiki/U.S._Route_127 "U.S. Route 127") designation from Lansing to Grayling.[[17]](#cite_note-ranzenberger-17) MDOT's stated reason for the modification was to "reduce confusion along the US 27/US 127 corridor".[[18]](#cite_note-27pr-18) After US 27's signage was removed, the highway north of the Lansing area was renumbered US 127, and the US 27 designation was removed from I-69.[[18]](#cite_note-27pr-18)

Some consolidation schemes involve the use of incorporating two single-digit numbers onto one marker, as along the [US 1/9](/wiki/U.S._Route_1/9 "U.S. Route 1/9") concurrency in northern [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey").[[19]](#cite_note-19) In the mid-20th century, [California](/wiki/California "California") had numerous concurrencies, but the [California Legislature](/wiki/California_Legislature "California Legislature") removed most of them in a [comprehensive reform of highway numbering in 1964](/wiki/1964_state_highway_renumbering_(California) "1964 state highway renumbering (California)").[[20]](#cite_note-renumbering-20)

## Regional examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concurrency_(road)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Regional examples")]

### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concurrency_(road)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: North America")]

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concurrency_(road)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Canada")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/QEW_and_Hwy_403_Concurrency.jpg/250px-QEW_and_Hwy_403_Concurrency.jpg)](/wiki/File:QEW_and_Hwy_403_Concurrency.jpg)

The [Queen Elizabeth Way](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_Way "Queen Elizabeth Way") concurrent with Highway 403 in Ontario

Concurrencies are also found in Canada. [British Columbia Highway 5](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_5 "British Columbia Highway 5") continues east for 12 kilometres (7.5 mi) concurrently with [Highway 1](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_1 "British Columbia Highway 1") and [Highway 97](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_97 "British Columbia Highway 97"), through [Kamloops](/wiki/Kamloops "Kamloops"). This stretch of road, which carries Highway 97 south and Highway 5 north on the same roadway (and vice versa), is the only wrong-way concurrency in British Columbia. Concurrencies are also very common in [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"). Most notably, the [Samuel-de-Champlain Bridge](/wiki/Samuel-de-Champlain_Bridge "Samuel-de-Champlain Bridge") features a concurrency with three Autoroutes: [A-10](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_10 "Quebec Autoroute 10"), [A-15](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_15 "Quebec Autoroute 15"), and [A-20](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_20 "Quebec Autoroute 20"). Another example is [A-55](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_55 "Quebec Autoroute 55"), which runs concurrently with A-10, A-20, and [A-40](/wiki/Quebec_Autoroute_40 "Quebec Autoroute 40"), all of which are major highways.

In [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), the [Queen Elizabeth Way](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_Way "Queen Elizabeth Way") and [Highway 403](/wiki/Ontario_Highway_403 "Ontario Highway 403") run concurrently between [Burlington](/wiki/Burlington,_Ontario "Burlington, Ontario") and [Oakville](/wiki/Oakville,_Ontario "Oakville, Ontario"), forming the province's only concurrency between two [400-series highways](/wiki/400-series_highways_(Ontario) "400-series highways (Ontario)").[[21]](#cite_note-MTO10-21) The concurrency was not in the original plan which intended for both the QEW and Highway 403 to run parallel to each other, as the Hamilton–Brantford and Mississauga sections of Highway 403 were initially planned to be linked up along a corridor (later planned to be [tolled](/wiki/Toll_highway "Toll highway")) now occupied by [Highway 407](/wiki/Ontario_Highway_407 "Ontario Highway 407"). To avoid forcing drivers to pay tolls to use a section of a continuous Highway 403, the new link was designated as a western extension of the tolled Highway 407, with the Mississauga section of Highway 403 planned to be renumbered as Highway 410. The renumbering to 410 never came to pass,[[22]](#cite_note-22) and consequently Highway 403 was signed concurrently along the Queen Elizabeth Way in 2002, remedying the discontinuity. Nonetheless, many surface street signs referring to that section of freeway with the QEW/Highway 403 concurrency still only use the highway's original designation of QEW, although the MTO has updated route markers on the QEW to reflect the concurrency.[[23]](#cite_note-concurrency-23)

At the national level, the [Trans-Canada Highway](/wiki/Trans-Canada_Highway "Trans-Canada Highway"), which does not bear a uniform number in the eastern provinces, follows various provincial highways. In the [Atlantic Provinces](/wiki/Atlantic_Provinces "Atlantic Provinces") the main designated TCH route either follows a single numbered route across each province (an exception being the switching of the designation between [Nova Scotia Highways 104](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_Highway_104 "Nova Scotia Highway 104") and [105](/wiki/Nova_Scotia_Highway_105 "Nova Scotia Highway 105")), or has branches that are signed exclusively as TCH routes. In Ontario and Quebec, The TCH follows a series of provincial highways, and also has branches that follow sections of others that have concurrencies with it, signed with TCH shields alongside the provincial number.

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concurrency_(road)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: United States")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Greensboro_road_signs.jpg/250px-Greensboro_road_signs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greensboro_road_signs.jpg)

An extreme example in [Greensboro, North Carolina](/wiki/Greensboro,_North_Carolina "Greensboro, North Carolina"), where [I-40](/wiki/Interstate_40_in_North_Carolina "Interstate 40 in North Carolina"), [I-85 Business](/wiki/Interstate_85_Business_(North_Carolina) "Interstate 85 Business (North Carolina)"), [US 29](/wiki/U.S._Route_29_in_North_Carolina "U.S. Route 29 in North Carolina"), [US 70](/wiki/U.S._Route_70_in_North_Carolina "U.S. Route 70 in North Carolina"), [US 220](/wiki/U.S._Route_220_in_North_Carolina "U.S. Route 220 in North Carolina"), and [US 421](/wiki/U.S._Route_421_in_North_Carolina "U.S. Route 421 in North Carolina") formerly ran concurrently

In the United States, concurrencies are simply marked by placing signs for both routes on the same or adjacent posts. The federal *[Manual on Uniform Traffic Control Devices](/wiki/Manual_on_Uniform_Traffic_Control_Devices "Manual on Uniform Traffic Control Devices")* prescribes that when mounting these adjacent signs together that the numbers will be arranged vertically or horizontally in order of precedence. The order to be used is [Interstate Highways](/wiki/Interstate_Highway "Interstate Highway"), [U.S. Highways](/wiki/U.S._Highway "U.S. Highway"), [state highways](/wiki/State_highway "State highway"), and finally [county roads](/wiki/County_road "County road"), and within each class by increasing numerical value.[[24]](#cite_note-MUTCD2D-24)

Several states do not officially have any concurrencies, instead officially ending routes on each side of one.[[a]](#cite_note-26) There are several circumstances where unusual concurrencies exist along state borders. One example occurs along the [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma")–[Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas") state line. At the northern end of this border [Oklahoma State Highway 20](/wiki/Oklahoma_State_Highway_20 "Oklahoma State Highway 20") runs concurrently with [Arkansas Highway 43](/wiki/Arkansas_Highway_43 "Arkansas Highway 43") and the two highways run north–south along the boundary.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concurrency_(road)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Europe")]

#### Mainland Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concurrency_(road)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Mainland Europe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Labsk%C3%BD_most%2C_zna%C4%8Dky_silnic.jpg/250px-Labsk%C3%BD_most%2C_zna%C4%8Dky_silnic.jpg)](/wiki/File:Labsk%C3%BD_most,_zna%C4%8Dky_silnic.jpg)

Concurrency of the city beltway, a European road, and three first-class roads in [Hradec Králové](/wiki/Hradec_Kr%C3%A1lov%C3%A9 "Hradec Králové"), Czechia

In a majority of the countries in Europe, the [European routes](/wiki/International_E-road_network "International E-road network") are signed concurrently with national roads. Exceptions include Belgium, Norway, Sweden and Denmark where only European route numbers are used for those roads. Other exceptions include the United Kingdom and Albania, where European routes are not signed at all.

In Sweden and Denmark, the most important highways use only the European route numbers that have cardinal directions. In Sweden the [European route E6](/wiki/European_route_E6 "European route E6") and [E20](/wiki/European_route_E20 "European route E20") run concurrently for 280 kilometres (170 mi). In Denmark the [E47](/wiki/European_route_E47 "European route E47") and [E55](/wiki/European_route_E55 "European route E55") run concurrently for 157 kilometres (98 mi). There are more shorter concurrencies. There are two stretches in Sweden and Denmark where three European routes run concurrently; these are E6, E20 and [E22](/wiki/European_route_E22 "European route E22") in Sweden, and E20, E47, and E55 in Denmark. Along all these concurrencies, all route numbers are posted with signs.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

In Czechia, the European route numbers are only additional, and they are always concurrent with the state route numbering, usually highways or first-class roads. In the state numbering system, concurrences exist only in first-class and second-class roads; third class roads do not have them. The local term for such concurrences is *peáž* (from the French word *péage*). In the road register, one of the roads is considered the main ("source") road and the others as the *péaging* (guest) roads. The official road map enables a maximum of five concurrent routes of the intrastate numbering system.[[28]](#cite_note-29) Cycling routes and hiking routes are often concurrent.

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concurrency_(road)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

In the United Kingdom, national routes do not run concurrently with others. Where this would normally occur, the roadway takes the number of only one of the routes (usually, but not always, the most important route), while the other routes are considered to have a gap and are signed in brackets (the equivalent of "to" signs in North America). An example is the meeting of the [M60](/wiki/M60_motorway_(Great_Britain) "M60 motorway (Great Britain)") and the [M62](/wiki/M62_motorway_(Great_Britain) "M62 motorway (Great Britain)") northwest of [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester "Manchester"): the motorways coincide for the seven miles (11 km) between junctions 12 and 18 but the motorway between those points is only designated as the M60 (although in this case the same junction numbers would also apply to the M62). [European route](/wiki/International_E-road_network "International E-road network") numbers as designated by [UNECE](/wiki/UNECE "UNECE") may have concurrencies (for instance [E15](/wiki/European_route_E15 "European route E15") and [E30](/wiki/European_route_E30 "European route E30") around [Greater London](/wiki/Greater_London "Greater London")), but since the E-route numbers are unsigned and unused in the UK, the existence of these concurrencies is purely theoretical.

### Middle East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Concurrency_(road)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Middle East")]

In Israel, two [freeways](/wiki/Freeway "Freeway"), the [Trans-Israel Highway](/wiki/Highway_6_(Israel) "Highway 6 (Israel)") (Highway 6) and [Highway 1](/wiki/Highway_1_(Israel) "Highway 1 (Israel)"), run concurrently just east of [Ben Shemen Interchange](/wiki/Ben_Shemen_Interchange "Ben Shemen Interchange"). The concurrency is officially designated "[Daniel Interchange](/wiki/Daniel_Interchange "Daniel Interchange")", providing half of the possible interchange directions. It is a one-mile (1.6 km) segment consisting of eight lanes providing high-speed access between the two highways. Access from Highway 1 west to Highway 6 south and Highway 6 north to Highway 1 east is provided via [Route 431](/wiki/Route_431_(Israel) "Route 431 (Israel)"), while access between Highway 1 east to Highway 6 north and Highway 6 south to Highway 1 west are provided at Ben Shemen Interchange. The other movements are provided through the concurrency.[[29]](#cite_note-30)
